ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rational design of efficient and stable photocatalysts has become an emerging research domain owing to its promising potential for solar fuel generation and environmental remediation. Among the different kinds of photocatalysts, Quantum dots (QDs) are acquiring considerable interest due to their unique properties, such as size-dependent bandgap, adjustable energy band levels, multi-exciton formation from single-photon absorption, and high surface area. Nevertheless, the existing challenges, including poor stability, wide bandgaps, and high recombination rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs continue to hinder their photocatalytic efficiency. QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions have emerged as high-profile photocatalysts, offering excellent light absorption and increased separation of high-energy photoinduced charge electron-hole pairs. The current review summarizes the photocatalytic properties of QDs, explores the basic concepts and mechanisms of S-scheme heterojunction, and discusses various QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts, including low bandgap QDs, wide bandgap QDs, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), for different photocatalytic applications. Moreover, it provides insights into future perspectives for designing effective QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts. The current review aims to provide a useful resource for scientists in designing high-performance S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts based on QDs and their composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the enhancing energy demand and environmental pollution, scientists have switched their focus toward designing sustainable and neat techniques for generating green energy and preserving the environment, including water pollution and global warming, all whilst maintaining living standards [1–3]. Renewable sunlight, with its infinite abundance, cleanliness, and sustainability, has emerged as a potential substitute for non-renewable fossil fuels for overcoming environmental challenges [4]. Among the different approaches for sunlight harvesting and conversion, photocatalysts, which drive chemical reactions via sunlight-semiconductor interactions, hold excellent potential to tackle both energy and environmental issues [5–7]. The performance of a photocatalyst depends upon the capability of a suitable semiconductor to become excited under sunlight irradiation, generating photoinduced electron-hole pairs with specific photon energy. These charge pairs subsequently undergo separation and migration to the CB and VB, respectively, to execute the redox reactions on the surface of the semiconductor catalyst [7–10]. For optimum performance under sunlight irradiation, the catalyst should demonstrate increased light absorption ability to maximize the formation and availability of electron-hole pairs. In addition, it should improve effective electron-hole separation and hold a high density of reactive sites to accelerate surface redox reactions. Hence, constructing an excellent photocatalyst with the above characteristics is necessary to obtain improved photocatalytic performance.

QDs, as 0D semiconductors, have obtained high interest for photocatalytic applications owing to their exceptionally small size (2–10 nm), often near the exciton Bohr diameter of the considered semiconductor [10,11]. Compared to conventional semiconductors, QDs hold a unique quantum size-dependent bandgap, allowing the modulation of their electronic and optical properties [12,13]. Consequently, by adjusting the composition, shape and size, the band structure of QDs can be modified, increasing redox potential and extending light absorption across the entire spectrum [14,15]. In addition, their high surface area facilitates a high number of active sites, significantly boosting the rate of redox reactions [16,17]. However, QDs suffer from challenges, including low stability and poor quantum yield, mainly owing to various factors, such as photocorrosion, non-radiative recombination from surface traps, and high recombination losses of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [18–20]. Furthermore, certain wide bandgap QDs exhibit limited light absorption and inadequate up-conversion behavior, which may impede their photocatalytic performance [21,22]. It is therefore of high interest to address these limitations to promote the photocatalytic performance of QDs photocatalysts.

To tackle the above challenges, QDs catalysts need to hold a more negative CB potential, a more positive VB potential, and the capacity to harvest extended light in the visible or NIR region. Understanding that both conditions cannot be acquired by a unitary catalyst, the construction of heterojunctions has become an emerging strategy. Heterojunctions provide extended light absorption and improved separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs simultaneously. Among these heterojunctions, S-scheme systems have earned promising attention due their capacity to improve the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, extend light absorption, and optimize redox potentials [22–24]. For readers interested in the fundamentals, limitations, benefits, and differences between charge migration mechanisms in type II, Z-scheme, and S-scheme heterojunctions, we suggest surveying our recently published review papers [25–27]. Matching the alignment of band structures and Fermi (Ef) levels between a QDs semiconductor and a second semiconductor is essential for constructing an S-scheme heterojunction. When two semiconductors are integrated, free electrons diffused from the semiconductor with a high Ef level to the semiconductor with a lower Ef level, resulting in the formation of a built-in electric field (BIEF) and band bending at the interface within S-scheme heterojunction, which consequently increases the separation of high-energy electron-hole pairs, enhancing overall performance [28–30]. The development of S-scheme heterojunction between QDs and other applicable semiconductors is an emerging research domain. Despite extensive attempts, the S-scheme heterojunction has yet to obtain noteworthy industrial breakthroughs, rendering it poise for fast development in the future.

To date, several excellent review papers have been published on the synthesis methods, properties, design, and photocatalytic applications of QDs. For instance, Sun et al. discussed the properties, types, and different photocatalytic applications of QDs in 2023 [18]. Rao et al. reviewed synthesis methods, the various roles of QDs in photocatalysis, and the use of different carbon QDs in photocatalytic applications [31]. Similarly, Cao et al. explored the role of QDs photocatalysts in biomass valorization [32]. In addition to these reviews, several other outstanding review studies have also been published on QDs-based photocatalysts during the previous few years. These excellent reviews serve readers with an in-depth understanding of the domain of QD photocatalysts. However, to the best of the authors' knowledge, none of the published reviews have covered QDs-based S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts. Hence, a timely and detailed discussion of the recent developments in this specific domain is urgently required.

Herein, we discuss the recent advances, challenges, research gaps, and future perspectives of QDs-based S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts. Notable emphases include characteristics of QDs, the fundamentals of S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts, and the classification of these heterojunctions into three types: small QDs-based S-schemes, wide bandgap QDs-based S-schemes, and GQDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts. The current review offers an in-depth understanding of interfacial interactions and charge migration processes, supporting researchers in the design and construction of unique QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts to expand their photocatalytic applicability.

In contrast to other nanostructured materials, QDs have been considered as one of the most promising materials for photocatalytic applications. Effective photoreaction needs three primary prerequisites: light absorption, the generation of electron-hole pairs, and rapid surface reactions. Obtaining these factors demands accurate manipulation of the nanostructure, electrical characteristics, and surface chemistry of the catalyst to promote photoactivity. As the size of QDs decreases, their surface area increases, which directly influences exciton characteristics, thereby increases the rate at which electron-hole pairs transfer to the reaction sites, hence accelerating the redox reactions [33,34]. Another important aspect of the quantum size impact is the reduction in bandgap value as QDs reduce in size, which consequently allows for the modulation of energy band positions. Notably, with smaller QDs, the CB potential becomes more negative, while the VB potential becomes more positive, providing an enhancement in the reduction and oxidation abilities that execute photocatalytic reactions [35–37]. By regulating the QDs size, the bandgap can be effectively controlled and, consequently, the light absorption ability of QDs can be conveniently adjusted. Compared to higher QDs size, smaller QDs size can increase the excitation energy of electron-hole pairs, improving the interfacial migration and separation of charge carriers, and hence are more viable to promote the photocatalytic performance. Similarly, the recombination loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs decreases significantly as the size of the QDs diminishes, due to an increased bandgap [38,39]. Thus, the optical properties of QDs can be adjusted by varying both their composition and particle size, attributed to the quantum size effect. By properly adjusting these factors, the light absorption response can be extended across the entire visible or even into the NIR region of the solar spectrum [40,41]. Notably, QDs display significant multi-excitation characteristics, providing the generation of more than one photoinduced electron-hole pair from single photon absorption. Meanwhile, due to 3D charge confinement, the electron and hole clouds are highly overlapped, which increases Coulomb interactions and oscillation power of photoinduced charge carriers, thereby enhancing the exciton stability [42,43]. Despite their multiple advantages, QDs offer several significant limitations, notably photocorrosion, which limits their photocatalytic activity and durability [44]. Furthermore, while reducing QD size can improve redox ability by increasing the bandgap, however, it results in the absorption of mainly UV light, thereby reducing the generation of electron-hole pairs and diminishing photoactivity [45,46]. In addition, despite their ability for multi-excitation per photon absorption, QDs often demonstrate inadequate photocatalytic efficiency due to substantial recombination loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [47]. To improve the photoactivity and stability of QDs, it is essential to overcome these challenges by preventing competitive electron-hole recombination and broadening the spectral response. One of the most efficient approaches is the design of a heterojunction because this permits the effective modulation of energy band levels through quantum size effects to simultaneously acquire a strong redox potential and extended light absorption, thereby improving the stability of QDs-based photocatalysts. In S-scheme heterojunctions, the aligned energy band levels of QDs and the coupled semiconductors provide the separation of high-energy photoinduced electrons and holes, thus improving the redox reactions and promoting photocatalytic performance, as discussed in the next section.

Yu et al. introduced the construction of an S-scheme heterojunction, consisting of two semiconductors with staggered band alignment, identical to a type II heterojunction, which provides a unique separation and migration of high-energy photoinduced electron-hole pairs [48]. In an S-scheme heterojunction, the coupled semiconductors are classified as either oxidation photocatalysts or reduction photocatalysts. As Fig. 1 (a) illustrates, the reduction photocatalyst has a more negative CB potential, a less positive VB potential, and a higher Ef level before contact. Contrarily, the oxidation photocatalyst has a less negative CB potential, a more positive VB potential, and a lower Ef level [49]. After contact, owing to differences in Ef levels of the two semiconductors, free electrons diffuse from the reduction photocatalyst to the oxidation photocatalyst in the dark state, with the latter receiving electrons and the former donating electrons, until the Ef levels of both semiconductors align at the interface [50,51]. The redistribution of free electrons leads to the creation of an electron-depleted region on the reduction photocatalyst side and an electron-enriched region on the oxidation photocatalyst side at the interface. Consequently, the oxidation photocatalyst acquires a net positive charge, whereas the reduction photocatalyst obtains a net negative charge, resulting in the generation of BIEF at the interface pointing from the oxidation photocatalyst to the reduction photocatalyst, as shown in Fig. 1 (b) [52]. Meanwhile, during the process of Ef level alignment between the two photocatalysts, the band levels of the reduction photocatalyst bend upward, whereas those of the oxidation photocatalyst bend downward near the interface [53,54]. Under light irradiation, both the oxidation photocatalyst and reduction photocatalyst are simultaneously excited, generating electrons and holes in their CB and VB, respectively. Because of the bent bands at the interface, the low-energy photoinduced electrons in the CB of the oxidation photocatalyst and the low-energy holes in the VB of the reduction photocatalyst are lost through recombination via electrostatic forces, as shown in Fig. 1 (c) [55]. Notably, band bending at the interface prevents the migration of photoinduced electrons from the CB of the reduction photocatalyst to the CB of the oxidation photocatalyst, and likewise, it blocks the migration of holes from the VB of the oxidation photocatalyst to the VB of the reduction photocatalyst. Thus, the construction of S-scheme heterojunction improves the separation of high-energy photoinduced electrons in the CB of the reduction photocatalyst and high-energy holes in the VB of the oxidation photocatalyst. This results in increased separation of high-energy electrons and holes between different semiconductors [56,57]. The combined influence of BIEF, bent energy bands, and electrostatic attraction plays the role of a driving force to promote the recombination of low-energy photoinduced electron-hole pairs while increasing the separation of high-energy electron-hole pairs across different components [58]. Importantly, the reduction photocatalyst contains isolated high-energy photoinduced electrons, which is favorable for solar fuel production. In contrast, the high-energy holes in the VB of oxidation photocatalysts within the S-scheme heterojunction are suitable for pollutant degradation [59]. Thus, for a photocatalytic solar fuel production, a reduction photocatalyst with a high reduction potential is preferred, as the oxidation potential of the oxidation photocatalyst can be sacrificed using a suitable holes scavenger [60]. Contrarily, for pollutant degradation, oxidation photocatalysts with a high oxidation potential and reduction photocatalysts with a high reduction potential are preferred [61]. In the case of solar fuel production, the photoinduced electrons in the CB of the reduction photocatalyst perform the reduction reaction, whereas holes in the VB of the oxidation photocatalyst are consumed with the assistance of sacrificial reagents [62]. For instance, these photoinduced electrons reduce H+ to generate H2 during hydrogen production, react with CO2 to generate different intermediates depending on their involvement in the CO2 reduction process, and reduce N2 to NH3 during nitrogen fixation [63]. In pollutant degradation, the high-energy photoinduced electrons in the CB of the reduction photocatalyst reduced oxygen to generate superoxide radicals, whereas high-energy holes in the VB of the oxidation photocatalyst oxidize water to produce hydroxyl radicals. These highly reactive radicals degrade pollutants into less harmful products [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Main cases of QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para:
Scientists have constructed plentiful S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts using different types of QDs, which have consequently shown excellent photocatalytic performances owing to improved light absorption, quenched recombination rates of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, and enhanced interfacial charge kinetics. When integrated with suitable semiconductors, the interfacial BIEF in S-scheme heterojunction can efficiently increase the separation of electron-hole pairs in QDs, promote overall charge carrier separation, and hence enhance the performance of the S-scheme photocatalyst. The versatility of QDs significantly expands the range of semiconductors that can be coupled via S-scheme heterojunctions, dramatically promoting photocatalytic efficiency compared to single-component catalysts. Because the photocatalytic process is driven by light-excited photoinduced electron-hole pairs for redox reactions, the extent of excitation in a photocatalyst highly depends on the bandgap and relative positions of the energy band levels of both the QDs and the coupled semiconductors within the S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst [65–68]. The proper alignment of energy band levels is pivotal for constructing S-scheme heterojunctions, which possess similar band structures and tunable interfacial interaction. Herein, we classify S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts into three categories based on their energy band levels and unique properties.
i.
large bandgap semiconductors integrated with small bandgap QDs
ii.
small bandgap semiconductors integrated with large bandgap QDs
iii.
semiconductors integrated with GQDs

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-scheme heterojunctions consisting of small bandgap QDs integrated with large bandgap semiconductors
ce:section.ce:para: The energy band levels play a pivotal role in controlling the photocatalytic performance of an S-scheme photocatalyst. The extent of band structure matching between QDs and other coupled semiconductors directly effects charge separation, migration, redox potential, and the thermodynamic requirements of the desired photocatalytic reaction [69,70]. Because of their suitable energy band levels, small bandgap QDs can develop energy band-aligned S-scheme heterojunctions with various large bandgap semiconductors, delivering a strong driving force for prolonged separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs at the interface [71]. To optimize the migration of photoinduced charge carriers in an S-scheme heterojunction, it's important to manage the band structure of the constituent semiconductors and pick appropriate carrier transport mediators, in addition to selecting materials with adequate energy band levels. For instance, Li et al. prepared oxygen vacancies (VO) enriched CeO2 with an adjustable density of surface VO, which was subsequently coupled with AgInS2 QDs to construct an S-scheme heterojunction, enabling controllable light absorption and excellent interfacial charge separation and migration [72]. Fig. 2 (a–g) illustrates the HRTEM and EDS results for AgInS2/CeO2, revealing that AgInS2 QDs, sized 3–5 nm, were effectively anchored onto the CeO2 matrix. The absorption spectra for pure catalysts and different AgInS2/CeO2-x heterojunctions are displayed in Fig. 2 (h). Compared to pure CeO2, the absorption edge of AgInS2/CeO2-x exhibits a red shift, ascribed to the quantum size effect of AgInS2 QDs, revealing increased photon absorption and electron-hole pairs generation, critical for redox reactions. The successful formation of VO was confirmed by XPS O 1 s analysis, which revealed a higher peak area for AgInS2/CeO2 than CeO2, as shown in Fig. 2 (i), owing to the enhanced surface VO density in the composite. Furthermore, the higher EPR signal of AgInS2/CeO2 compared to CeO2, as shown in Fig. 2 (j), verified the formation of VO with increased density, serving as electron trappers to improve photocatalytic performance. To evaluate the inhibition of electron-hole recombination and increased interfacial charge separation and migration abilities, the photocatalysts were examined using PL and photocurrent techniques. In Fig. 2 (k), the AgInS2/CeO2-x catalyst revealed a lower PL intensity than CeO2 and AgInS2, which could be attributed to enhanced charge generation and separation, owing to the synergistic effects of quantum confinement, the role of VO as charge trappers, and the creation of an S-scheme. Fig. 2 (l) revealed that AgInS2/CeO2-x electrodes exhibited higher current compared to pure CeO2 and AgInS2, demonstrating the compelling benefits of integrating low bandgap AgInS2 QDs with high bandgap CeO2 for photocatalytic application. The optimized catalyst showed the highest production rate of 60 % for xylonic and 3689.9 μmolh−1g−1 for CO generation, which was attributed to the high separation of high-energy electron-hole pairs through an S-scheme heterojunction between AgInS2 QDs and CeO2. The in-situ XPS (ISIXPS) was used to verify the S-scheme charge migration mechanism, as shown in Fig. 2 (m–o). Unlike ex-situ XPS, ISIXPS revealed that binding energies of Ce 3d orbitals showed a positive shift, while those of Ag 3d and In 3d exhibited a negative shift. This shift offers evidence that photoinduced electrons moved from CeO2 to AgInS2, supporting the S-scheme mechanism, as shown in Fig. 2 (p). The creation of the interfacial BIEF and S-scheme mechanism was further validated using KPFM and AFM techniques, as shown in Fig. 2 (q-t). Under dark conditions, a potential difference of 51 mV between AgInS2 QDs and CeO2 validated the formation of interfacial BIEF, pointing from AgInS2 QDs to CeO2. In addition, under light irradiation, the potential of AgInS2 QDs reduced by 45 mV, compared to 13 mV for CeO2, identifying the flow of photoinduced electrons from CeO2 to AgInS2, and further validating the S-scheme mechanism. Hence, it can be concluded that the creation of VO effectively improves light absorption and charge separation in the S-scheme heterojunction, significantly promoting the interfacial charge kinetics. Despite the excellent potential of AgInS2 QDs, there are limited studies on their application in S-scheme heterojunctions for solar fuel generation. At this end, AgInS2 QDs warrant higher attention in future research, notably for use in hierarchical S-scheme heterojunctions to avoid aggregation and obtain improved photocatalytic results. Moreover, an in-depth understanding of S-scheme charge transfer mechanisms is required, which needs advanced experimental techniques, including ISIXPS and KPFM, as well as DFT computations to obtain deeper insights into interfacial charge kinetics and photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic performance of S-scheme heterojunctions is optimized by retaining a suitable content of QDs. When the QDs content exceeds a critical level, the photocatalytic performance suffers due to the negative effects, including light shielding or blocking of reactive sites, which can induce electron-hole recombination [73]. For instance, in Zhang's research, SnS2 QDs anchored onto ZnIn2S4 nanosheets were synthesized to explore the quantitative effect of SnS2 QDs on light absorption, electron-hole separation, interfacial charge kinetics, and their correlation to photoactivity for H2 generation [74]. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the HRTEM image of ZnIn2S4/SnS2 reveals SnS2 QDs (4 nm) were uniformly dispersed on the surface of ZnIn2S4 nanosheets, which were nearly 50 nm thick. The corresponding absorption spectra are displayed in Fig. 3 (b). As the mass of SnS2 QDs in the heterojunction was increased from 5 to 20 wt%, the absorption shoulder of the composite extended towards higher wavelengths. This suggests that the creation of an S-scheme heterojunction effectively increased light absorption due to the synergistic interaction between wide bandgap ZnIn2S4 and low bandgap SnS2. PL and TRPL studies were used to determine the separation efficiency and lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in ZnIn2S4 nanosheets and SnS4 QDs within the S-scheme heterojunction. Fig. 3 (c) reveals that different ZnIn2S4/SnS4 composites showed lower PL intensity than pure ZnIn2S4, with ZIS/SnS2-10 % catalyst exhibiting the lowest intensity. This result indicates that coupling SnS2 QDs with ZnIn2S4 nanosheets impeded the electron-hole recombination rate through S-scheme heterojunction. As shown in Fig. 3 (d), the optimized ZIS/SnS2-10 % photocatalyst demonstrated faster decay kinetics than pure ZnIn2S4, suggesting enhanced charge separation ability when integrating SnS2 QDs with ZnIn2S4 nanosheets. The charge lifetime of pure ZnIn2S4 was 3.27 ns, whereas the ZIS/SnS2-10 % obtained a higher lifetime of 5.64 ns, indicating a higher electron-hole separation across the S-scheme interface. The above observations were further validated by the EIS Nyquist plots, as shown in Fig. 3 (e), which verified that anchoring SnS2 QDs onto ZnIn2S4 nanosheets through a robust interfacial contact lowered interfacial charge migration resistance. The ZnIn2S4/SnS4 composites showed higher photocatalytic performance compared to pure ZnIn2S4, reflecting the useful influence of anchoring SnS2 QDs. Of the three composites, the optimized ZIS/SnS2-10 % exhibited the highest H2 generation rate at 1.13 mmolh−1g−1, which was 16.14 and 2.4 times higher than pure ZnIn2S4 and ZIS/SnS2-20 %, respectively. Excessive anchoring of SnS2 QDs (20 wt%) in the heterojunction resulted in increased electron-hole recombination and reduced active sites, ultimately diminishing the photocatalytic performance. Hence, exploring the optimal amount of QDs in the S-scheme heterojunction is critical for obtaining excellent photocatalytic results. A potential S-scheme route for interfacial carrier migration is shown in Fig. 3 (f). Owing to a higher Ef level of ZnIn2S4 (- 5.05 eV) than SnS2 (- 6.65 eV), free electrons moved to SnS2 in dark conditions, resulting in band bending and BIEF at the interface, directed from ZnIn2S4 to SnS2. Upon light irradiation, the retained electrons in the CB of ZnIn2S4 reduced protons to generate H2, whereas the holes in the VB of SnS2 were oxidized by TEOA. However, the authors didn’t verify the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism in the ZnIn2S4/SnS2 heterojunction using ISIXPS and KPFM techniques. Future research must include these techniques to acquire a deeper understanding of electron-hole separation and migration across the interface. In addition, in this research, ZnIn2S4 nanosheets of 50 nm thickness must be further optimized to enhance the excellence of S-scheme heterojunction. Furthermore, to enhance the excellence of S-scheme heterojunctions, more attention should be given to novel and sophisticated anchoring methods to obtain robust interfacial contact between QDs and coupled semiconductors. Notably, DFT computation should be prioritized in determining the Ef level, strength, and robustness of interfaces to design high-quality S-scheme heterojunctions.

Recently, there has been widespread attention on encapsulating low bandgap QDs within the pores or channels of wide bandgap semiconductors to create S-scheme heterojunctions. This technique not only protects the QDs from photocorrosion but also maximizes the composite's potential for photocatalytic reactions by utilizing the synergistic effects of both semiconductors. For instance, in Dong's research, CsPbBr3 perovskite QDs were successfully encapsulated in the mesopores of TiO2 beads (MTB), benefiting from the quantization effect of QDs on interfacial carrier kinetics [75]. The resulting S-scheme heterojunction was expected to demonstrate improved photocatalytic performance for CO2 reduction, as the stability of the photocatalyst was increased through the encapsulation of CsPbBr3 QDs. Fig. 4 (a,b) illustrates the analyses from TEM and HRTEM for the CsPbBr3/MTB composite. The results revealed that the composite maintained the nanostructure bead morphology of MTB, reflecting the successful encapsulation of CsPbBr3 QDs within the mesopores of MTB. This observation was further validated by HRTEM examination, which indicated the lattice spacing of 0.352 and 0.58 nm for the TiO2-101 and CsPbBr3-110 surfaces, respectively, hence authenticating the successful fabrication of a heterojunction composite. Fig. 4 (c, d) shows the absorption spectra and corresponding bandgap results for both pure and composite catalysts. Notably, as the content of CsPbBr3 QDs in the heterojunction increased, the absorption spectra of the composites showed a red shift in the light-harvesting compared to MTB, which was ascribed to the synergistic effect of two semiconductors. Importantly, the Ef level of CsPbBr3 QDs (- 0.61 eV) was significantly higher than that of MTB (0.21 eV), reflecting a staggered band structure that is necessary for the creation of S-scheme heterojunction. To elucidate the electron-hole separation and migration behaviors of the S-scheme heterojunction, photocurrent response and TRPL studies were used. In Fig. 4 (e), the CsPbBr3/MTB composite showed unique decay kinetics compared to the pure CsPbBr3 QDs. The observed charge lifetime for CsPbBr3/MTB and CsPbBr3 were 45.94 and 19.13 ns, respectively. The higher lifetime of charge carriers in CsPbBr3/MTB compared to pure CsPbBr3 revealed that the heterojunction effectively improved the separation of electron-hole pairs. This observation was further validated by the increased current density of CsPbBr3/MTB composites, as shown in Fig. 4 (f), reflecting that the formation of the CsPbBr3/MTB heterojunction significantly promoted the separation and migration of electron-hole pairs. The optimized CsPbBr3/MTB showed excellent 4.64 and 3.49 times higher CO2 conversion performance in contrast to pure MTB and CsPbBr3 QDs, respectively. This enhancement in performance could be attributed to the formation of an S-scheme heterojunction between MTB and CsPbBr3, as shown in Fig. 4 (g). The S-scheme heterojunction effectively retained high-energy electrons and holes in the CB of CsPbBr3 and VB of MTB, providing the reduction and oxidation reactions essential for CO2 conversion. Furthermore, the construction of a mesoporous surface significantly enhanced the contact area, allowing for the rapid transfer of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. For future research, the construction of 3D mesoporous nanostructures, including microspheres or microflowers is suggested to develop QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions. These heterojunctions would increase interfacial contact with the deposited QDs, thereby enhancing the interfacial charge kinetics, and consequently resulting in improved photocatalytic performance.

Facet engineering has obtained high interest for its potential to rationally control the facets of coupled semiconductors in QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions. Different facets of the same semiconductor reveal varying electronic properties, resulting in the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs with variable redox potentials [76]. Consequently, the semiconductor facet that develops an interface through solid-solid interaction with QDs can highly affect the interfacial carrier migration that happens once the photoinduced electron-hole pairs migrate from the lattice to the interface. For instance, Dong et al. used an in-situ technique to anchor CdS QDs, characterized by exposed (100) facets, onto the (110) facets of the InVO4 atomic layer, exploring their application in photocatalytic degradation of ethylene [77]. The mass ratios of CdS QDs to InVO4 nanoflakes in the heterojunction were adjusted from 5 % to 40 % to evaluate the effect of interfacial separation and migration of photoinduced charge carriers on the photocatalytic performance. Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the TEM image of CdS QDs/InVO4, revealing that the heterojunction was successfully fabricated. The corresponding absorption spectra were displayed in Fig. 5 (b), exhibiting that the integration of InVO4 with CdS QDs resulted in a red shift in light absorption, which promoted the generation of electron-hole pairs. By comparing the PL emission spectra of different catalysts, insights into electron-hole recombination at the CdS QDs/InVO4 interface were deduced. Fig. 5 (c) shows the PL spectra of pure CdS QDs, InVO4, and CdS QDs/InVO4 composite. The composite catalyst revealed the lowest PL emission intensity, reflecting that interfacial charge migration was improved due to the formation of heterojunction between CdS QDs and InVO4, thereby lowering the recombination loss of electron-hole pairs. Further, TRPL spectra, characterized by rapid and slow initial decay kinetics, were used to explore the interfacial carrier kinetics between CdS QDs and InVO4. As shown in Fig. 5 (d), the results revealed that CdS QDs/InVO4 heterojunction demonstrated an average charge lifetime of 27 ns for rapid and 915 ns for slow initial decay kinetics. These values were much higher than rapid lifetime of pure InVO4 (11 ns) and slow lifetime CdS QDs (714 ns), suggesting excellent interfacial charge migration that provides high separation of electron-hole pairs. This increased lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs was further verified by enhanced degradation performance of CdS QDs/InVO4 heterojunction, which revealed 13.8 and 13.2 times higher degradation rates than CdS QDs and InVO4, respectively. This enhancement was attributed to the effective separation of high-energy electron-hole pairs through the S-scheme heterojunction mechanism between CdS QDs and InVO4, which accelerated the redox reactions. The ISIXPS analysis was used to authenticate the S-scheme charge transfer route between CdS QDs and InVO4, as shown in Fig. 5 (e–h). In comparison to pure CdS, the binding energies of Cd 3d and S 2 s orbitals in the CdS QDs/InVO4 heterojunction revealed a positive shift under dark conditions, followed by a negative shift upon illumination. Similarly, the binding energies of In 3d and V 2p orbitals in the CdS QDs/InVO4 heterojunction shifted negatively and positively, respectively, under dark and light illumination conditions, compared to pure InVO4. This suggested photoinduced electrons migrated from InVO4 to CdS, consistent with the S-scheme migration mechanism. EPR analysis further validated the S-scheme charge migration between CdS QDs and InVO4, as shown in Fig. 4 (i, j). The optimized composite showed both DMPO-
• O
2 −
and DMPO-•OH signals, in contrast to CdS and InVO4, which exhibited only DMPO-
• O
2 −
and DMPO-•OH signals, respectively. This reflected that the CdS QDs/InVO4 heterojunction retained photoinduced electron-hole pairs with high redox potentials, supporting the S-scheme carrier transfer mechanism. The formation of QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions through facet engineering has shown great potential for improving photocatalytic performances. However, the authors measured the stability tests only for three cycles, and photocorrosion of CdS could decrease the photocatalytic performance over prolonged irradiation, a challenge that must be tackled in future research. To design S-scheme systems, it is significant to consider important factors, including matching band structures and lattice parameters, the crystallinity of semiconductors, and the quantization effect of QDs. In addition, many studies report the utilization of capping agents during the synthesis process to control the orientational growth of semiconductors. However, this frequently results in a heterojunction designed by interfacial contact of the interface, which can lower the interfacial charge kinetics and decrease photocatalytic performance. Future research should emphasize using surfactants, ligands, and template-assisted growth techniques to better control the facets of semiconductors in QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-scheme heterojunctions consisting of large bandgap QDs integrated with small bandgap semiconductors
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of wide bandgap QDs with narrow bandgap semiconductors via S-scheme heterojunctions has been widely explored to increase photocatalytic performances. Obtaining optimum matching of the energy band levels of semiconductors and QDs in such heterojunctions is critical for providing the interfacial separation and migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [78]. Preferably, one component (either semiconductor or QDs) should contain higher reduction potentials, while the other should demonstrate higher redox potentials. This generates an effective potential difference between the bands of the heterojunction, resulting in a stronger driving force for charge separation. Furthermore, a robust interface between the QDs and semiconductor is necessary because strong bonding between components in S-scheme heterojunction effectively lowers the interfacial charge migration resistance, thereby improving charge kinetics and enhancing overall photocatalytic performance [79]. The interfacial strength can be increased by adjusting the construction mode of the interface between components in a heterojunction. For instance, Liang et al. adopted an in-situ coating technique to anchor CdS QDs onto ZIF-67, forming an intimate interfacial contact that highlighted the beneficial influence of intimate interfacial contact on the separation and migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs in the constructed S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst [80]. ZIF-67 (denoted as ZM) was prepared using various solvents, namely methanol and DMF, indicated as ZMT and SDT, respectively. Moreover, the CdS QDs content in the heterojunction was adjusted from 20 % to 60 % to evaluate its effect on photocatalytic activity. As Fig. 6 (a) illustrates, the small-sized QDs were uniformly dispersed and highly anchored onto ZM. Fig. 6 (b) displays a comparison of the light absorption potentials of CdS, ZM, and different composite catalysts. Compared to pure ZM, the CdS/ZM composites exhibited extended light absorption in both UV and visible regions, reflecting a higher density of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, which executed the surface redox reactions. To explore the separation and migration of charge carriers, photocurrent density, and EIS Nyquist plots were measured. As shown in Fig. 6 (c), the CdS/ZM composite showed a significantly higher current density compared to CdS and ZM, indicating the pivotal role of heterojunction in improving the separation and migration of photoinduced carriers. In Fig. 6 (d), the CdS/ZM composite displayed a smaller arc radius than pure CdS QDs and ZM, revealing the compelling benefits of integrating CdS QDs with ZM by reducing interfacial resistance and providing smoother carrier transfer. These observations align well with higher photocatalytic results observed for the CdS/ZM composite. Notably, compared to pure ZIF-67, the photocatalytic performance exhibited an increment of over 1.9 times, revealing the outstanding effect of S-scheme construction between CdS QDs and ZIF-67. In addition, M-S plots revealed that CB potentials for ZIF-67 and CdS were– 0.11 and – 0.83 eV vs. NHE. Based on VB-XPS results, the VB potentials of CdS and ZIF-67 were determined to be 2.17 and 1.41 eV, respectively. Hence, the CdS/ZIF-67 composite was proposed to show an S-scheme band alignment. The photoinduced electron in ZIF-67 and holes in CdS recombined owing to the BIEF, resulting in a situation where photoinduced electrons and holes were separately preserved at CdS and ZIF-67, respectively, to execute the redox reactions, as shown in Fig. 6 (e). In the future, using different morphologies of ZIF-67 to construct S-scheme heterojunctions with QDs could be useful. The efficiency of these heterojunction systems can be further optimized by adjusting the particle size of QDs. Varying the particle size of QDs results in the modification of their band positions or Ef level, which improves the potential difference between band structures of QDs and coupled semiconductors, which consequently serves as a driving force to enhance the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs at the heterojunction interface. Once the optimum particle size of QDs in the heterojunction has been obtained, adjusting the composition of the coupled semiconductor should be considered. This modification can modify the band positions or Ef level of the coupled semiconductor, further enhancing the potential difference between QDs and the semiconductor to fine-tune the driving force. Another effective approach involves modifying the electronic structures of wide bandgap QDs by introducing dopants, which create additional electronic states, opening innovative channels for carrier transition and enhancing charge kinetics across the heterojunction interface to improve charge separation and photocatalytic activity.

The interface contact plays a critical role in determining the photocatalytic performance of QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions, as it provides the migration of photoinduced charge carriers across the interface. When the interface contact area is small, charge migration rates reduce, resulting in high recombination of electron-hole pairs [81]. The construction of nanoflowers provides a solution by offering a higher contact area and multiple anchoring sites, effectively immobilizing the QDs. This results in a robust S-scheme interface between semiconductors and QDs, which not only enhances the separation of electron-hole pairs but also improves the density of exposed active sites for redox reactions. For instance, Li et al. anchored BiOBr QDs onto Bi7O9I3 nanoflowers and explored the effect of varying contents (10−100) of BiOBr QDs in the heterojunction on the photocatalytic performance for O2 generation [82]. Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the SEM image of the BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 composite, revealing that BiOBr QDs were effectively dispersed across Bi7O9I3 nanoflowers. The PL spectra were used to explore the separation ability of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), both pure BiOBr and Bi7O9I3 demonstrated higher PL intensity in contrast to BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 composites, suggesting increased charge separation was endowed by the formation of the heterojunction. Fig. 7 (c) shows the decay dynamics of BiOBr, Bi7O9I3, and BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 catalysts. The charge lifetimes of BiOBr and Bi7O9I3 were measured to be 0.85 ns and 1.24 ns, respectively. In contrast, the charge lifespan of the BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 heterojunction increased to 1.84 ns, revealing extended separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, which was useful for redox reactions. This characteristic further promoted the photocatalytic performance. The optimized 50-BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 catalyst exhibited a 3.1 and 1.6-fold enhancement in O2 generation compared to BiOBr QDs and Bi7O9I3 nanoflowers, respectively. DFT and UPS calculations revealed that the work function of Bi7O9I3 was higher than that of BiOBr, permitting the migration of free electrons from BiOBr to Bi7O9I3 in the dark. The EPR analysis further reflected that no DMPO-
• O
2 −
signal was observed for Bi7O9I3, whereas both BiOBr and BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 exhibited DMPO-
• O
2 −
signal owing to their appropriate reduction ability. Notably, the BiOBr/Bi7O9I3 catalyst showed the highest intensity for both DMPO-
• O
2 −
and DMPO-•OH signals in contrast to its competitors, as shown in Fig. 7 (d, e). This revealed that photoinduced electron-hole pairs with strong potential were retained within the heterojunction, consistent with the S-scheme charge migration mechanism, as shown in Fig. 7 (f). In the future, it is necessary to investigate the optimum composition of BiOBr QDs by integrating nanoflowers of different semiconductors to further understand the differences in the charge kinetics and reveal the importance of selecting appropriate semiconductors for the rational design of QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions. Moreover, graphene QDs with optimized contents in S-scheme systems should be deeply explored and compared with semiconductor QDs at lower contents to obtain inexpensive semiconductor QDs with significantly increased photocatalytic performances. In addition, the underlying carrier transfer route in these heterojunctions should be explored using advanced techniques such as in-situ XPS, FTIR, KPFM, and DRIFTS, to obtain deeper insights for the development of efficient QDs-based S-scheme heterojunction systems.

The construction of QDs-based core-shell S-scheme heterojunctions has recently gained popularity due to their superior performance and the ability to enhance the stability of QDs through protective shells. Typically, a semiconductor with a large band gap and chemical resilience semiconductor is employed as the shell in these core-shell heterojunctions [83]. This large band gap shell acts as an energy obstruction, isolating the wave function of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and keeping the QDs safeguarded from external influences. For instance, Li et al. explored the photocatalytic performance for H2 production by constructing a core-shell S-scheme heterojunction featuring g-C3N4 QDs as the core and CdS as the shell [84]. In addition, Er:WO3 nanorods were introduced into the core-shell heterojunction to provide increased charge transfer channels, further improving the stability of the catalysts. As displayed in Fig. 8 (a), the SEM image shows that CdS, with a shell thickness of 250 nm, effectively covered all g-C3N4 QDs in Er:WO3/CdS/g-C3N4 heterojunction, thereby providing the interfacial migration of charge carriers. As Fig. 8 (b) illustrates, light absorption increased with the doping of Er into WO3, followed by the integration of Er:WO3 with a CdS shell. However, the absorption of Er:WO3/CdS decreased upon integration with the g-C3N4 QDs core. The introduction of Er dopant into the WO3 lattice altered its electronic characteristics, which was expected to improve the absorption properties of WO3. Subsequently, modifying CdS with Er:WO3 further shifted light harvesting due to the narrow bandgap of CdS (2.32 eV). Interestingly, the absorption response reduced after integrating ErWO3/CdS with g-C3N4 QDs. This revealed that the integration of wide bandgap g-C3N4 QDs with low bandgap CdS reduced the light absorption ability of the S-scheme heterojunction. However, the photocatalytic performance of Er:WO3/CdS/g-C3N4 for H2 production was 20 and 62 times higher than that of CdS and g-C3N4, respectively, indicating that the improved performance was not solely assigned to increased light absorption. Moreover, the optimized photocatalyst showed a small loss of 4.66 % in H2 evolution performance even after six successive runs, revealing the strong stability of the catalyst. To examine the separation and interfacial migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, PL and EIS Nyquist studies were used. In Fig. 8 (c), all unitary catalysts exhibited increased PL intensity compared to Er:WO3/CdS/g-C3N4, which was assigned to an intimate interfacial contact facilitated by the heterojunction, which consequently reduced the radiative recombination and increased the interfacial charge migration. This characteristic was further confirmed by the EIS Nyquist results shown in Fig. 8 (d). Pure CdS and g-C3N4 showed a higher arc radius, reflecting larger charge transfer resistance, resulting in the loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs owing to recombination. In contrast, the useful role of Er:WO3 is evident in its prerequisite of abundant charge transfer channels and the formation of an Er:WO3/CdS/g-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction through a robust interfacial contact. This configuration effectively diminished the interfacial charge migration barrier, resulting in increased separation of electron-hole pairs. The work function of CdS (4.62 eV) was higher than that of Er:WO3 (3.74 eV) and g-C3N4 QDs (3.69 eV), revealing that under dark conditions, free carrier moved from Er:WO3 and g-C3N4 to CdS in Er:WO3/CdS/g-C3N4 system, resulting in the generation of BIEF and band bending at the interface. Based on the proposed mechanism, the photoinduced electrons in the CB of Er:WO3 recombined with holes in the VB of CdS, while the photoinduced electrons in g-C3N4 migrated to CdS, resulting in increased separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The cumulated electrons at CdS reduced H+ to generate H2, while the holes in Er:WO3 were scavenged by the sacrificial reagent. However, the authors didn’t verify the S-scheme charge transfer mechanism using advanced techniques, such as ISIXPS, EPR, and KPFM, which should be included in future research. However, the construction of QDs-based core-shell S-scheme photocatalysts can demonstrate higher photocatalytic performance owing to increased light absorption, quenched recombination loss of photoinduced electro-hole pairs, and an enhanced density of reaction centers. However, the H2 production performance of these systems, like that of other photocatalysts, heavily relies on the use of sacrificial reagents, which severely limits their commercial viability. As a result, future research may focus on diminishing the need for sacrificial reagents to increase the commercial application of QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts.

Despite several benefits of constructing QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts, several inherent limitations persevere. One significant limitation is the difficulty in accurately modulating the band edge potentials of QDs within S-scheme photocatalysts [85]. To align the energy band levels of QDs with those of the integrated semiconductor, it is essential to optimize the photochemical characteristics of QDs, which is complex, as it entails maintaining the size of QDs and managing their surface coordination conditions [86]. For instance, Bi et al. demonstrated that the creation of surface VO in TiO2 QDs could effectively modulate their band offsets to match with those of g-C3N4 nanosheets, thereby providing the construction of an S-scheme heterojunction and resulting in increased photocatalytic performance for H2 production [87]. The composite was subjected to heat treatments at different temperatures (100–300 ⁰C) in a nitrogen environment. EPR spectra exhibited that the density of VO increased with temperature due to the effective elimination of surface lactate species. The TiO2 heated at 300 ⁰C was named TiO2-VO. FTIR analysis showed variations in the positions of characteristics vibrations of N-H and Ti-O-Ti bonds in the TiO2/g-C3N4 composite compared to single TiO2 and g-C3N4, indicating a strong interaction between TiO2 QDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets. As Fig. 9 (a) illustrates, the absorption edge of TiO2 was extended from 391 to 418 nm with an increase in temperature and further shifted to 461 nm for TiO2-VO/g-C3N4 photocatalyst. This revealed that the formation of heterojunction effectively reduced the excitation energy of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The combined Mott-Schottky and bandgap results revealed that, in comparison to the CB and VB potentials of TiO2 QDs, which were - 0.89 and 2.28 V, respectively, the CB and VB potentials of TiO2-VO QDs were modified to - 0.67 and 2.30 V, demonstrating the effect of VO on band edge positions. Moreover, the CB and VB potentials of g-C3N4 were measured to be – 0.93 and 1.8 V, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 (b–e), XPS analysis was used to verify the interaction and charge migration direction within the TiO2-VO/g-C3N4 photocatalyst. Under dark conditions, the C 1 s and N 1 s orbitals in TiO2-VO/g-C3N4 composite showed a positive shift compared to g-C3N4, while the binding energy of Ti 2p and O 1 s exhibited a negative shift relative to TiO2, revealing that electrons were migrated from g-C3N4 to TiO2 in the heterojunction. In contrast, these orbitals showed an adverse shift in binding energy under light irradiation, verifying that photoinduced electrons moved from TiO2-VO to g-C3N4. This resulted in the formation of an S-scheme charge migration route between the two materials. These observations were aligned with H2 production results, which showed that the optimized TiO2-VO/g-C3N4 photocatalyst obtained the highest performance of 337 μmolg−1h−1, making 10 times enhancement than g-C3N4. In addition, the cycling tests revealed a small loss in H2 production performance after six cycles, confirming the strong stability of the prepared photocatalyst. To further confirm the formation of the S-scheme mechanism, EPR analysis was used to examine the charge migration direction under visible light illumination. As Fig. 9 (f, g) shows, no EPR DMPO-
• O
2 −
and DMPO-•OH signals were detected for TiO2 QDs, as their CB and VB potentials were not enough to meet the required potential for O2/
• O
2 −
(- 0.33 V) and OH¯/•OH (1.8 V), respectively. In contrast, the EPR analysis of TiO2-VO revealed both DMPO-
• O
2 −
and DMPO-•OH signals, assigned to their higher potentials compared to standard potentials of the redox reactions. Notably, pure g-C3N4 only revealed DMPO-
• O
2 −
signal owing to its lower CB potential (- 0.93 V) than O2/
• O
2 −
(- 0.33 V). Significantly, both O2/
• O
2 −
(- 0.33 V) signals were observed in the TiO2-VO/g-C3N4 composite, verifying that photoinduced electrons and holes were respectively separated at g-C3N4 and TiO2-VO, consistent with the S-scheme charge migration mechanism, as shown in Fig. 9 (h). The separated electrons at g-C3N4 reduced H+ to generate H2, whereas holes at TiO2-VO oxidized by the sacrificial reagent.

Besides, the construction of QDs-based p-n S-scheme heterojunction is considered as an excellent approach for significantly increasing photocatalytic performance. This system influences the capacity of p-n S-scheme heterojunction to effectively impede the recombination loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [88]. For various photocatalytic applications, the p-n junction can be constructed by coupling n-type or p-type QDs with semiconductors of the opposite conductivity type. In Amasegowda’s research, a p-n S-scheme heterojunction was successfully designed, including p-type Ni3V2O8 QDs (NVQ) and n-type ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (UCN) to examine the photocatalytic activity for H2 production under visible light illumination [89]. The FTIR spectra of UCN showed characteristic signals at 808 and 892 cm-1, corresponding to the vibrational modes of the s-triazine ring and the disordered structure resulting from the distortion of cross-linked heptazine. These peaks showed variation in the NVQ/UCN composite, as shown in Fig. 10 (a), indicating a strong interaction between NVQ and UCN. As Fig. 10 (b) demonstrates, the EDAX spectra of the NVQ/UCN composite exhibited the distribution of Ni, O, V, N, and C elements, validating the formation of the composite. The SEM image in Fig. 10 (c) exhibits that QDs are enveloped by nanosheets, resulting in robust interfacial contact, which facilitated plentiful reaction centers and provided rapid carrier migration. This observation was further supported by the increased surface area of the NVQ/UCN composite, which enhanced from 94 to 162 cm2/g, decreased the charge diffusion distance and increased light absorption due to the interaction of the sp-d correlation contact between CB electrons of UCN and localized d-electrons of Ni ions. Consequently, the optimized NQV/UCN composite showed a maximized H2 production rate of 3380 μmolh−1g−1, displaying enhancement of 14.1 and 3.97 times compared to NQV and UCN, respectively. Notably, there was a negligible loss in H2 production performance and no structural changes after 16 h of successive recycling cycles, validating the strong chemical stability of the photocatalyst. Furthermore, the authors determined the p-type and n-type conductivity of NVQ and UCN through their negative and positive Mott-Schottky slopes, respectively. Due to variations in Ef levels, free electrons diffused from n-type UCN to p-type NVQ upon contact, leading to band bending and the formation of BIEF at the interface, as shown in Fig. 10 (d). Under light irradiation, low potential photoinduced electrons in the CB of NVQ and low potential holes in the VB of UCN recombined at the interface, improving the separation of high potential electrons and holes in UCN and NVQ, respectively. The photoinduced electrons in the CB of UCN reduced H+ to produce H2, whereas the holes in the VB of NVQ oxidized water to produce O2. Hence, the construction of QDs-based p-n S-scheme heterojunction shows significant potential for photocatalysis. However, conventional techniques demonstrate the variability in Ef levels, potential differences, and the corresponding charge migration mechanism in these systems. However, there has been no emphasis on using advanced techniques such as ISIXPS, KPFM, EPR, and fs-TAS to gain deeper insights into charge migration mechanisms. Future research is expected to delve into the band structure, potential differences, and corresponding charge transfer mechanisms in QDs-based p-n S-scheme heterojunctions.

To overcome lattice matching issues, the construction of S-scheme homojunction has gained recently widespread attention owing to their efficient charge separation abilities. By integrating similar materials with varying morphologies and energy band levels, these heterojunctions obtain outstanding inherent lattice matching and continuity of band bond interactions, providing the enhanced separation and migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [90]. For instance, Pournemati et al. designed a homojunction S-scheme photocatalyst that integrated TiO2 QDs (TQD) and brown TiO2 nanoparticles (BTO) to explore the combined effects of QDs and S-scheme junction on photocatalytic performance for N2 fixation [91]. The TiO2 QDs, size less than 10 nm, were effectively integrated with TiO2 nanoparticles ranging from 2 to 9 nm in the TQD/BTO composite. The results in Fig. 11 (a) exhibit a negative shift in the (004) and (204) diffraction planes of BTO and TQD/BTO compared to TQD, revealing an increase in lattice dimension due to the formation of Ti+3 and VO. This observation was further validated by Raman spectra, where the Eg band at 144 cm−1 showed a positive shift in BTO and TQD/BTO compared to TQD. The creation of Ti+3 sites and VO BTO effectively modulated its electronic and optical properties, improving its light absorption response compared to TQD, as shown in Fig. 11 (b). Moreover, the formation of a homojunction between TQD and BTO further enhanced light absorption, creating additional photoinduced electron-hole pairs and accelerating redox reactions. As shown in Fig. 11 (c, d), both EIS and PL studies revealed that the combination of homojunction formation and defect creation effectively reduced the interfacial charge migration resistance and inhibited the recombination of charge carriers. This increased separation of electron-hole pairs indicated an extended lifetime, which was beneficial for improving photocatalytic performance. This observation aligned with the photocatalytic results, which revealed that the optimized TQD/BTO catalyst obtained a maximum N2 fixation performance of 11844 μmolL−1g−1, illustrating a 4.46 and 3.07 times enhancement over TQD and BTO, respectively. Furthermore, no significant decrease in N2 fixation ability was observed after six cycles, with the optical and structural properties remaining unchanged, revealing the high stability of the photocatalyst. Mott-Schottky analysis verified the n-type conductivity of both materials, with TQD showing a more negative CB potential (- 0.40 V) relative to BTO (- 0.1 V). This revealed that the formation of VO and Ti+3 defects increased the reducibility of BTO compared to TQD. Moreover, the Ef level of TQD was higher than that of BTO, providing free electron migration from TQD to BTO, which resulted in the generation of interfacial BEIF and band bending at the interface. Under light irradiation, low-energy electrons in the CB of BTO and low-energy holes in the VB of TQD recombined at the interface, improving the separation of high-energy electron-hole pairs. Electrons in the CB of TQD reacted with N2 to produce NH2, while holes in the VB of BTO oxidized water into H+ and oxygen. The enormous potential of QDs-based homojunction S-schemes in photocatalysis is huge; however, their application remains highly limited to laboratory research. To increase the photocatalytic performance, further investigation of novel QDs-based homojunction using advanced in-situ techniques is required to optimize the photocatalytic efficiency. Specifically, optimizing the chemical compositions of components in S-scheme homojunction photocatalysts is critical, as it can be controlled to modify energy band levels or modulate the Ef level, promoting the separation and transfer of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Further, future research should focus on examining the optimal constituent content in QDs-based S-scheme homojunction, as excessive amounts of either component can have negative effects, such as light shielding, blocking of reaction centers, and reduced interfacial charge migration. Size optimization of QDs in S-scheme homojunction is critical, as size variation directly affects the energy band positions and bandgap of photocatalysts. This optimization can increase both redox potential and light absorption, ultimately promoting photocatalytic performance. Moreover, exploring the defect manipulation in QDs-based homojunction S-scheme photocatalysts is necessary. Understanding the influence of defects on charge dynamics will disclose whether they serve as charge trap sites or recombination sites, hence influencing overall photocatalytic performance.

GQDs have recently obtained outstanding interest in the construction of S-scheme photocatalysts owing to their excellent photocatalytic properties. With a size of −2–10 nm and a thickness of 1–2 nm, GQDs exhibit exceptional light absorption abilities and PL spectra, both of which are the main prerequisites for photocatalysis [91–93]. The quantum confinement and edge effects permit GQDs to modulate their bandgap from 0 eV to 6 eV, making them appropriate for matching the energy band levels of integrated components in S-scheme systems [94,95]. Moreover, variations in particle size not only suitably modify the band edge positions but also promote the density of oxygen functionalities to serve as effective reaction centers, thus promoting the overall photocatalytic performance of the QDs-based S-scheme system [96]. The high surface area of GQDs provides reduced charge migration routes, minimizing the recombination loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [97]. Hence, combining GQDs with suitable semiconductors to develop an S-scheme heterojunction is an excellent approach to promote photocatalytic performance. For instance, Liu et al. anchored GQDs onto BiOBr to explore the quantitative effect of GQDs on the photocatalytic properties of BiOBr for RhB degradation [98]. The GQDs/BiOBr mass ratio was adjusted from 1 % to 10 %. As shown in Fig. 12 (a, b), SEM and TEM images of the GQDs/BiOBr composite revealed that dispersion of ultrathin GQDs nanosheets onto BiOBr retained the flower-like morphology of the composite, as the small size of GQDs offered plentiful available reaction centers and highly reduced carrier migration distances. In addition, in Fig. 12 (c–f), XPS analysis verified the successful construction of the composite and the strong interaction between GQDs and BiOBr. Compared to BiOBr, an additional peak in the XPS C1s spectrum at 288.54 eV, attributed to the formation of the Bi-O-C bonds, verified the strong electronic interaction between the components. In addition, the spectra of the composite also revealed the emergence of oxygen functionalities, including CO and –OH from GQDs, which participated in the creation of a robust interfacial interaction, supporting the rapid migration of charge carriers. The corresponding PL and EIS Nyquist results are shown in Fig. 12 (g, h). PL spectra revealed that a mass ratio of 7 % was optimal for quenching the recombination loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, due to the formation of an S-scheme junction between GQDs and BiOBr. However, PL analysis also revealed that exceeding a 7 % mass ratio posed an adverse effect on the separation ability of electron-hole pairs. The strong interfacial interaction was further verified by EIS Nyquist results, which exhibited that optimized GQDs/BiOBr-7 composite showed the lowest interfacial charge transfer resistance, revealing the correlation between mass content and photocatalytic performance. Mott-Schottky and bandgap results revealed that the CB and VB potentials of the composite were – 0.40 and 2.33 V, respectively, verifying their suitability for redox reactions. Under visible light irradiation, the optimized GQDs/BiOBr-7 composite obtained 100 % RhB degradation in 140 min, which was 3.26 times higher than that of pure BiOBr. Furthermore, the performance of the optimized photocatalyst reduced to 85.17 after five cycling tests, revealing its effective stability. This was further validated by no structural variation in the XRD analysis. The work function of GQDs (4.87 eV) was lower than that of BiOBr (5.43 eV), indicating that, in the dark state, free electrons migrated from GQDs to BiOBr. Consequently, the energy bands of GQDs bent upward, while those of BiOBr bent downward at the interface. Under light irradiation, the photoinduced electrons at BiOBr and holes at GQDs recombined, improving the separation of electron-hole pairs between the two semiconductors following the S-scheme mechanism, as shown in Fig. 12 (i). The superoxide radicals were generated through the interaction of high-energy electrons in GQDs with oxygen, while high-energy holes at BiOBr oxidized water to generate hydroxyl radicals. These radicals subsequently degraded the pollutants into water and CO2. Hence, GQDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts revealed a high potential for pollutant removal. However, current research has mainly focused on organic pollutants, with negligible emphasis given to the degradation of heavy metal ions. Future studies should focus on the removal of heavy metals, including lead, mercury, silver, etc. to broaden the scope of application of GQDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts. Furthermore, due to their unique photocatalytic abilities, GQDs should be combined with LDHs and MOFs to construct more efficient S-scheme photocatalysts. Evaluating their performance for fuel production and N2 fixation remains a promising but unexplored domain.

To further increase the photocatalytic activity of GQDs-based S-scheme heterojunction systems, n-type heteroatom doping can be incorporated into the lattice of GQDs. This doping modulates the electronic characteristics of GQDs, with dopants playing the role of charge activation centers that promote the separation and migration of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the dopants facilitate additional electrons and introduce extra energy levels between π and π* orbitals of C, decreasing the band gap and enhancing light-harvesting, which consequently promotes the overall photocatalytic performance of the S-scheme system [99]. In Tang’s research, N and S co-doped GQDS (NS-GQDs) were anchored onto the surface of TiO2 nanospheres to increase the photocatalytic activity for toluene removal under visible light irradiation [100]. FE-SEM analysis, as shown in Fig. 13 (a), verified the successful distribution of NS-GQDs onto the urchin-like structure of TiO2 nanospheres, while retaining the original morphology of TiO2 nanospheres. The EDS analysis in Fig. 13 (b) further revealed the uniform distribution of S, Ti, O, N, and C elements across the NS-GQDs/TiO2, verifying the uniform dispersion of NS-GQDs on the TiO2 surface. Moreover, pore size distribution analysis exhibited pore diameters in the range of 10–15 nm, indicating the porous structure of NS-GQDs/TiO2, which allowed for plentiful reactive sites and provided excellent charge migration channels for rapid diffusion of reactants to reactive sites. Raman spectra revealed a small red shift in the D and G band peaks of NS-GQDs relative to GQDs, identifying that heteroatom-doping effectively distorted the sp2 symmetry of C and improved the disordered structure. The slight negative shift revealed in the Eg(1) characteristics peak of TiO2 in NS-GQDs/TiO2, compared to pure TiO2 spectra, along with two additional peaks corresponding to GQDs, as shown in Fig. 13 (c), indicated a strong interaction between TiO2 and NS-GQDs. This interaction was further verified by XPS analysis, which revealed a blue shift in the binding energy of the Ti 2p orbitals in GQDs-coupled TiO2, relative to pristine TiO2, revealing an enhanced charge density for TiO2 owing to a strong interaction between GQDs and TiO2, as shown in Fig. 13 (d). In addition, the O 1 s spectra comparison demonstrated the appearance of an additional peak matching to Ti-O-C at the sacrifice of oxygen peak at 532.6 eV in GQDs-coupled TiO2 composites, as shown in Fig. 13 (e), further validating the electronic interaction between TiO2 and GQDs. Unlike the absorption spectra of TiO2, a significant red shift in light absorption, from 397 nm to 442 nm, was attributed to the electronic interaction between NS-GQDs and the CB of TiO2. By NS doping, extra electrons were incorporated into the carbon π-network, which reduced its durability and provided the orbital overlap between GQDs and (N, S) dopants. This resulted in the creation of new energy states between π and π* bands, significantly lowering the bandgap and widening the light absorption response, as shown in Fig. 10 (f). To examine the charge separation and migration properties, PL and EIS Nyquist techniques were used. As Fig. 13 (g) shows, the PL spectra of NS-GQDs/TiO2 composite not only showed a significant reduction in emission intensity but also indicated a red shift in the emission peaks compared to pure TiO2. The decreased PL intensity revealed that the recombination loss of photoinduced electron-hole pairs was effectively suppressed, assigned to the high mobility of NS-GQDs. Meanwhile, the red shift in emission peaks was associated with a decreased bandgap, resulting from the generation of intermediate states between π and π* orbitals owing to electron donor dopants. This observation was further validated by the EIS Nyquist results, as shown in Fig. 13 (h), which revealed that the introduction of high conductivity in the heterojunction effectively lowered the interfacial charge migration resistance, promoting the separation and migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The photocatalytic results of the prepared materials aligned with these observations, with the optimized NS-GQDs/TiO2 composite obtaining a toluene degradation rate of 99.8 % within 6 h. Moreover, Mott-Schottky and DRS studies revealed that the CB potentials of NS-GQDs and TiO2 were – 0.52 and – 0.34 V, whereas their VB potentials were 1.76 and 2.78 V, respectively. According to the proposed S-scheme mechanism, low-energy photoinduced electrons in TiO2 and low-energy holes in NS-GQDs recombined, providing the increased separation of high-energy electrons and holes in NS-GQDs and TiO2. These charges participated in the generation of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, which efficiently degraded the toluene, respectively, as shown in Fig. 13 (i). The ISIXPS analysis verified the successful construction of an S-scheme between NS-GQDs and TiO2. Under light irradiation, the binding energies of Ti 2p and O 1 s orbitals shifted positively compared to those under dark conditions, while the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s, and S 2p orbitals shifted negatively. This verified that photoinduced electrons migrated from TiO2 to NS-GQDs, aligning with the S-scheme mechanism. Currently, porous structures are widely used to anchor GQDs due to their capacity to form powerful interfacial contact and promote charge transfer. However, these porous structures can suffer from excessive anchoring of GQDs, which can shield the pores and diminish the interfacial contact area, ultimately decreasing photocatalytic efficiency. In the future, higher emphasis should be focused on the development of alternative useful structures, such as spherical and Janus configurations, to establish structure diversity and enhance GQDs anchoring efficiency. Furthermore, the application scope of GQDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions should be expanded beyond organic pollutants to include heavy metals, H2 production to CO2 conversion, and N2 fixation.

Besides, p-type heteroatom doping in GQDs can effectively increase their quantum yield by modulating their electronic characteristics and creating novel energy levels near the Ef level [101]. These levels effectively trap photoinduced electrons, promoting electron-hole separation and adjusting the band positions to reduce the bandgap and extend the light absorption. Consequently, the photocatalytic performance of the S-scheme system is significantly enhanced [102]. For example, in Liu's research, an in-situ growth process was employed to anchor B-doped GQDs (B-GQDs) onto bismuth molybdates (BMO) to develop a heterojunction system, followed by exploring its photocatalytic performance for N2 fixation [103]. Fig. 14 (a) shows the TEM image of the B-GQDs/BMO composite, obviously exhibiting the successful integration of B-GQDs with BMO through a robust interfacial contact. XPS analysis was used to examine the interfacial interaction between B-GQDs and BMO. As Fig. 14 (b-d) displays, the binding energies of Bi 4 f, Mo 3d, and O 1 s orbitals exhibited a positive shift in B-GQDs/BMO composite compared to BMO, authenticating the robust interfacial interaction and migration of free electrons from B-GQDs to BMO. Further confirmation of the interaction between B-GQDs and BMO was given by a red shift in light absorption, from 502 nm to 508 nm, as shown in Fig. 14 (e), resulting in a reduced bandgap and improved migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Based on the combined results of optical bandgap and VB-XPS analysis, the CB potential of B-GQDs and BMO was found to be – 0.89 and 0.68 V, respectively, whereas the VB potentials were 2.81 and 1.71 V. The lifetime of photoinduced electron-hole pairs was determined using TRPL spectra. As shown in Fig. 14 (f), the B-GQDs/BMO showed faster decay kinetics than BMO. The average carrier lifetime for B-GQDs (7.76 ns) was shorter than BMO (13.57 ns), implying that charge separation was obtained through lower photon energy and subsequently trapped at the reactive sites, reducing charge recombination losses, which, in turn, increased the photocatalytic performance of B-GQDs/BMO. The B-GQDs/BMO photocatalyst achieved an effective N2 fixation rate of 157.04 μmolh−1g−1 and maintained its performance over five cycling experiments, demonstrating both its excellence and stability. Under light irradiation, the photoinduced electrons in the CB of BMO and the holes in the VB of B-GQDs recombined, resulting in increased separation of high electrons at B-GQDs and holes at BMO, as shown in Fig. 14 (g). The photoinduced electrons at B-GQDs reduced N2 to NH3, whereas holes at BMO oxidized water to oxygen and protons.

Besides, the integration of piezocatalytic semiconductors with GQDs in S-scheme systems has been shown to improve the separation and migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic performance [104]. For instance, in Ning’s work, GQDs were integrated with piezoelectric semiconductor ZnO to construct an S-scheme heterojunction, obtaining remarkable photocatalytic removal of MO [105]. As shown in Fig. 15 (a–d), TEM and EDS studies verified the successful distribution of GQDs on the surface of ZnO. Raman spectra further verified the successful preparation of GQDs/ZnO composite, with characteristic D and G bands of GQDs emerging at 1342 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to disordered symmetry and the in-plane vibration of sp2-conjugated network of C. Further corroboration was obtained from a higher shift in the typical E2 high peak of ZnO in GQDs/ZnO composite, compared to pure ZnO, as shown in Fig. 15 (e), revealing a strong interaction between the GQDs and ZnO. XPS technique facilitated further validation of the interfacial interaction within the composite. As Fig. 15 (f, g) shows, the blue shift in the binding energy of Zn 2p and O 1 s orbitals for the GQDs/ZnO composite, compared to ZnO, showed an enhancement in the electron density of ZnO. This verified a robust interfacial contact between the GQDs and ZnO, ascribed to the development of the GQDs/ZnO composite, which provided electron migration from the GQDs to ZnO. Fig. 15 (h) displays the C 1 s spectra, identifying Zn-C-O in the GQDs/ZnO composite, signifying a powerful chemical bond at the composite's interface. This allowed for excellent charge migration while retaining structural durability. KPFM analysis revealed a piezoelectric potential of 27.6 mV, authenticating that GQDs/ZnO demonstrated larger piezoelectricity than ZnO. These observations were consistent with photocatalytic results, which showed that the GQDs/ZnO photocatalyst obtained an outstanding MO degradation rate of 96.1 % within 1 h, which was 2.53 times higher than that of ZnO. In addition, cycling experiments revealed that the degradation rate decreased from 96.1 % to 91.3 % after four cycles, with no observed variation in the structure or morphology, validating the strong stability of the prepared GQDs/ZnO photocatalyst. Based on DFT computations, the work function values for GQDs, ZnO, and the GQDs/ZnO were measured to be 5.24, 5.28, and 4.99 eV, respectively, verifying free electrons migration from GQDs to ZnO. The combined results from the optical bandgap and VB-XPS analysis revealed the CB/VB positions for GQDs and ZnO as –1.57/1.39 and –0.30/2.86 V, respectively. EPR analysis further verified the formation of an S-scheme charge migration mechanism between GQDs and ZnO. As Fig. 15 (i, j) illustrates, the spectra for ZnO showed no DMPO-
• O
2 −
signal, but a significant DMPO-•OH signal was witnessed, which could be assigned to negative CB potential (- 0.3 V) of ZnO than O2/
• O
2 −
(- 0.33 V) and its more positive VB potential than OH¯/•OH (1.99 V). Similarly, the detection of DMPO-
• O
2 −
signal and absence of DMPO-•OH signal in the spectra for GQDs was attributed to its sufficiently negative reduction potential (- 1.57 V), facilitating the generation of superoxide radicals (O2/
• O
