, - 0.33 V), and its lower VB potential (1.39 V) than OH¯/•OH (1.99 V). Notably, both DMPO-
• O
2 −
and DMPO-•OH signals were significantly increased in the GQDs/ZnO spectra relative to those of GQDs and ZnO. This revealed an enhanced separation of high energy electrons and holes in the CB of GQDs and VB of ZnO, aligned with the S-scheme charge migration mechanism, as shown in Fig. 15 (k). The formation of the Zn-O-C interfacial bond served as a bridge, providing electron migration from GQDs to ZnO under dark states, which resulted in the generation of BIEF and band bending at the interface. Mechanical stress induced by ultrasonication further polarized ZnO owing to the piezoelectric effect, increasing the strength of BIEF. Under light irradiation, photoinduced electrons in the CB of ZnO and holes in the VB of GQDs recombined, driven by the synergistic influence of BIEF and band bending, leading to increased separation of high-energy electrons at GQDs and holes at ZnO. The high energy electrons at GQDs reduced oxygen to generate superoxide radicals, whereas the holes in ZnO oxidized water to produce hydroxyl radicals. The radicals subsequently converted the MO pollutant into less harmful products, such as water and CO2. To date, except ZnO, no other piezoelectric semiconductor has been used to develop QGDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions for piezo-photocatalysis. Hence, the exploration of additional piezoelectric semiconductors in the field of piezo-photocatalysis is highly anticipated. Further, advancements in synthesis methods are anticipated, as present methods, such as the hydrothermal method, are relatively conventional. Future research should also focus on introducing reliable standards for chemical composition, catalyst amount, pollutant concentration, and ultrasonic treatment parameters, including power and frequency.

QDs photocatalysts have attracted significant attention in various photocatalytic applications owing to their tunable and size-dependent bandgap, facilitating extended light absorption, high surface area to provide plentiful reactive sites and excellent electron-hole pair generation under light irradiation. However, the photocatalytic performance of QDs is often limited by inadequate quantum yield and poor stability. Integrating QDs with suitable semiconductors to construct S-scheme heterojunction has been found effective in minimizing these limitations, thereby increasing photocatalytic performance. The performance of QDs-based S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts can be further optimized by modulating the bandgap through the adjustment of energy band levels by controlling facets, morphology, and defects. Further, optimizing factors, including temperature-controlled crystallinity, the quantitative content, and the composition of the composite components can effectively extend light absorption, reduce electron-hole pair recombination, and increase the redox capacity. These modifications cooperatively lead to higher photocatalytic activity in QDs-based S-scheme heterojunctions. The quality of the S-scheme interface can be improved by increasing the surface area and doping heteroatoms into GQDs to create chemical bonds with the coupled semiconductor. This strategy enhances the contact area and interaction between components, thereby improving charge separation and overall photocatalytic performance. Despite considerable progress in the development of QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts, this dopamine is still in its infancy. Further optimization and progress in this emerging research direction are required, as suggested below.
1.
Most of the reported studies have used conventional techniques to characterize the basic properties such as light absorption, charge separation, and redox potentials, which are frequently utilized to justify the increased photocatalytic performance of QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts. However, negligible attention has been devoted to exploring the interactions between the orbitals of components and charge migration mechanisms at the interface between semiconductors using advanced techniques, such as STM, ISIXPS, KPFM, fs-TAS, and DRIFTS. To overcome this research gap, further research efforts are required to enhance the understanding of orbital interactions in semiconductors, with the target of developing robust interfacial contact and elucidating the charge migration and separation mechanisms. The process involves the separation and migration of photoinduced electron-hole pairs, in addition to the buildup and loss of carriers in the dark state, which should be explored under actual-time operational circumstances to map and measure parameters, including potential difference and interfacial BIEF, band bending, and energy band alignment.
2.
QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts are mostly employed for pollutant removal, with limited application in solar fuel generation. This highlights the need to broaden their applications to include photocatalytic H2 production, CO2 reduction, and N2 fixation. Furthermore, most current studies focus on the photocatalytic performance of a single pollutant, despite the complexity of real water, which mostly contains multiple pollutants. Hence, more emphasis should be focused on overcoming the degradation of various pollutants simultaneously rather than focusing on single pollutants.
3.
Another significant consideration is that the light source utilized in the ongoing research can be underestimated. Most research used artificial lamps to excite QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts, whose intensity, and thus charge generation ability, differs highly from the natural sunlight. This reveals that the performance evaluations of these photocatalysts can be completely validated. Hence, while using an artificial light source and a single pollutant might reveal ideal experimental conditions for QDs-based photocatalysts, their actual photocatalytic performance remains unexplored. This requires further examination into the effects of natural sunlight and complex pollutant environments on the photocatalytic performance of QDs-based photocatalysts.
4.
Defects, such as oxygen vacancies, can modify energy band positions in QDs-based S-scheme photocatalysts. It is necessary to further investigate how the density and location of these defects affect band edge potentials and interfacial interactions to maximize charge carrier separation and migration. While a large content of defects can increase photocatalytic performance, it can also provide limitations, including enhanced charge recombination centers, unintended shifts in band positions, and reduced charge mobility, ultimately diminishing the overall performance of S-scheme photocatalysts.
